items.translationZh:
标题：📊 PRO：本周可视化报告

正文：欢迎阅读《他们是如何赚钱》的星期六专业版。超过30万订阅者依赖我们的商业和投资洞见。若您错过了：🚀《SpaceX如何赚钱》🔍《谷歌为何出售价值850亿美元的股份》立即订阅。Premium会员可获得：📈 每月报告：200 多家公司的可视化分析。📰 星期二文章：独家深度剖析与见解。📚 访问我们的档案库：数百篇商业拆解。PRO 会员可获全部内容加：📰 星期六 PRO 报告：对最新财报的及时洞察。

今天概览：
☀️ Oracle：积压订单 vs. 资产负债表 🎵 Adobe：免费增值赌注 ✍️ DocuSign：IAM 占 ARR 的 13% 🎖️ Vail Resorts：创纪录最差季节 🐶 Chewy：压力大但稳定

1️⃣ ☀️ Oracle：积压订单 vs. 资产负债表
Oracle 本季收入超过预期，利润也超预期，且积压订单增加 8500 亿美元，至 6380 亿美元。但股价仍下跌 12%，创下自去年十二月以来最差单日表现。问题不在于本季业绩，而在于即将到来的账单。新任 CFO Hilary Maxson 指引 FY27 毛利率将“下降”，并公布约 700 亿美元的净现金支出，其中约 400 亿美元将通过新融资获得。投资者自然会审视将这些积压订单转化为收入的成本。

Q4 FY26 关键数据：
- 收入同比增长 21% 至 192亿美元（超出 10亿美元预期）。
- Oracle 云基础设施（OCI）仍为主要驱动力：☀️ 云业务同比增长 47% 至 99亿美元（OCI 同比增长 93% 至 58亿美元）。
- 软件业务同比下降 2% 至 68亿美元。
- 硬件业务同比增长 9% 至 9亿美元。
- 服务业务同比增长 13% 至 15亿美元。
- 运营利润率持平在 32%，毛利率因低利润率 IaaS 规模化下降 5 个百分点至 65%。
- 现金流方面，FY26 全年运营现金流为 320亿美元（同比增长 54%），但不足以支撑资本支出提升。自由现金流仍深度为负（-240亿美元），因为资本支出达 560亿美元，超过 500亿美元计划。
- 资产负债表方面，Oracle 在 FY26 融资 430亿美元债务和 50亿美元股权，预计 FY27 再融资约 400亿美元债务与股权。Oracle 现在净债务为 1240亿美元，包含经营租赁负债。

来源：Fiscal.ai

FY27 指引：管理层重申 FY27 收入指引约 900亿美元，暗示约 34% 增长。资本支出预计约 700亿美元，全部由 Oracle 自有现金支付。Oracle 预计将筹集约 400亿美元的债务与股权融资，任何新债务可能推迟至 2027 年日历年。

那么我们该如何解读？
🔈 市场忽视的亮点：RPO（未实现收入订单）同比增长 363%，环比增长 15%，至 6380亿美元，远超分析师预期约 5900亿美元。但市场已不再仅为积压订单买单，开始计价实现这些订单的成本。
🔧 融资压力稍减，但需求仍然巨大：约 750亿美元的 Oracle 大型 AI 合同已预付或由客户提供硬件，这有助于缓解。但 FY27 的资本支出仍然庞大，Oracle 仍需外部融资。融资情况有所改善，但绝对数字更大。
📊 利润率成为新痛点：Oracle 正在先行建设产能后再计费，导致资金支出先行、收入滞后。管理层称，一旦利用率提升，基础设施利润率会快速恢复，但投资者仍需看到实际证明。
👴 老的 Oracle 正在缩小：软件收入同比下降 2%。Oracle 仍需依赖这部分传统现金流为 AI 基础设施建设提供资金，而收入基数的收缩使得整体数学更为困难。

要点：Oracle 在 FY26 交付了 1.2 吉瓦的新增数据中心容量，预计 FY27 Q1 交付将接近 1 吉瓦。看涨情形愈发具体，但资本密集度、利润率压力和融资需求也同样凸显。Oracle 现在更像是对执行力的押注。

2️⃣ 🎵 Adobe：免费增值赌注（后续内容略）

items.author: https://www.generalist.com/feed
items.title: Introducing Generalist Intelligence
items.textEn: Friends, For several years, I’ve wanted to launch a weekly intelligence briefing. While there are exceptional private intelligence groups covering the financial markets and geopolitics, and many great tech publications, I nevertheless found myself wanting something a little different from what I was able to find. Something that delivered an elegant, insightful analysis of the hidden shifts and fresh opportunities bubbling up in our industry. Something high-signal, low-fluff, and with, perhaps, a dash of wit. Something that provides a little greater situational awareness in thirty minutes or less. But doing it right would require taste and craft, as well as real time. For an intelligence product to succeed, it must find fresh angles on known stories and the non-obvious ones just bubbling beneath the surface. That takes scrounging and scouring. Earlier this year, we were able to unlock both. Most important was convincing one of my favorite writers, an old hand at this kind of work, to jump aboard and lead the charge. By leveraging frontier models, we’ve been able to build an extensive signal-gathering apparatus that would not have been possible to conduct without a very large team, spanning news, industry outlets, social media, academic research, government and regulatory filings, code repositories, model-adoption aggregators, fundraising data, prediction markets, website monitoring, open-source intelligence sources, and talent flows. We also monitor a select group we refer to as "super signalers" across various verticals, selected by us, who we believe are frequently ahead of the curve. Not all of these approaches are bearing fruit yet, but we expect them to improve as we build and fine-tune. Already, we’ve seen the benefits of a process that searches so expansively. Not only does it surface tidbits a human might have missed, but it spots echoes or juxtapositions that are revealed only with capacious context. However, the story selection and writing are done by us, the humans. The system hunts ceaselessly, bringing up a furious exhaust of data, noise, and signal, all in one. It is up to the human to think, judge, analyze, and write. Generalist Intelligence is the result of this marriage, a weekly intelligence briefing designed for tech’s most discerning professionals. It will arrive Friday mornings. Our hope is that it opens you up to perspectives you hadn’t considered and offers a look at fresh pockets of the future you might have missed. I’ve been reading internal versions of this as we tuned the format over the past couple of months, and have found myself looking forward to it every week. Subscribe now We’re in the very early stages of getting this right, and there’s a long way to go. We’d love to know what you think, especially what you’d like to see more of, as it comes up. Our goal is for this to become the highest signal-to-noise newsletter you receive each week, and one worth setting aside the time for. Now, onto the briefing. — Mario With SpaceX blasting into the public markets and Anthropic and OpenAI straightening their ties to follow suit, the bajillion-dollar question is, obviously: are they worth it? Anyone claiming to know the answer very likely has a bridge to sell you. Nevertheless, we can’t stop thinking about it. SpaceX feels like a special case. Public markets are being asked to price in total interstellar domination at IPO , with a good trillion of the $1.77 trillion price tag – at which the loss-making company would trade at over 90x last year’s revenue – based on a series of highly speculative goals including repeat business on Mars, data centers in orbit, and making a key contribution to the development of AI. On the other hand, it’s Elon. So, you know. What’s really got our chins wagging at Generalist Towers this week is a more existential question. Anthropic and OpenAI haven’t filed public prospectuses, but they look likely to be priced as though they are in, effectively, a two-horse race for control of the future (ok, maybe Google too). Some of us think that’s right – that it’s only a matter of time before one of the model labs cracks recursive self-improvement, and then it’s a short hop to AGI and either Sam or Dario as World King. But a couple of things have landed in the past week that have got us thinking seriously about the other side. So we thought it would be fun to take the contrarian stance, and make the bear case for AI. AI, huh, what is it good for? LLMs are incredibly good at coding. At the end of last week, Anthropic dropped an essay titled When AI builds itself , in which the Claude-maker claimed that more than 80% of the code it merges into its codebase was written by Claude, and that its engineers were shipping "8x as much code per quarter" compared to their relatively flat productivity between 2021 and 2025. (When I asked Claude about this, it raised an eyebrow at the figures, pointing out they were "self-reported by a company currently raising money on exactly this story.") And to be fair, rather gallantly, the good folks at Anthropic point out that the 8x figure is likely an overstatement as it measures "quantity over quality." But the direction is clear: Claude is incredible at writing code. Presumably, this is translating into giant gains for all? Well, no. A new study of more than 100,000 GitHub developers found that Claude Code allowed coders to create or edit almost 300% more files. But that uplift was halved to 150% by the time they got to submitting pieces of work for review, and that in turn shrunk a further 5x by the time it got to shipping. In other words, coders using the latest agentic AI tools released just 30% more software, compared to those raw-dogging it. The researchers attribute that remaining 30% to what they call "strong complementarities" between the agents and the coders, meaning the agent very much needs the coder. They also noted that while there was a marked uptick in new apps being made this way, there was "no increase" in overall demand. This doesn’t mean agents are bad at code, but it does mean they might not be nearly as good as you thought at the thing they’re best at. So what happens to the model makers? Read more
items.summaryZh: 作者宣布推出名为“Generalist Intelligence”的周度情报简报，旨在通过广泛的数据搜集和人工分析，为技术行业专业人士提供高信号、低噪声的洞见，帮助投资者捕捉行业中的潜在趋势和机遇。
items.translationZh:
标题：通用情报（Generalist Intelligence）

正文：朋友们， 多年来，我一直想推出一份每周情报简报。虽然已有出色的私人情报机构覆盖金融市场和地缘政治，也有许多优秀的科技出版物，但我仍希望能找到与现有资源略有不同的东西。希望呈现出对行业中潜在的转变和新兴机会进行优雅、洞察性的分析。希望内容高信号、低噪声，或许还带点机智。希望在三十分钟或更短时间内提供更高的情境感知。但要做好这件事，需要品味、工艺以及实时性。情报产品要想成功，必须在已知故事上找到新角度，并挖掘那些尚未显现的潜在信息。这需要大量的搜集与筛选。 今年早些时候，我们实现了这两点。最关键的是说服了我最喜欢的作者之一——一位在此类工作方面经验丰富的老手——加入并领衔。通过利用前沿模型，我们建立了一个广泛的信号收集体系，这在没有庞大团队的情况下是无法实现的，团队覆盖新闻、行业媒体、社交媒体、学术研究、政府和监管文件、代码仓库、模型采用聚合器、融资数据、预测市场、网站监测、开源情报源以及人才流动等。我们还监测一组我们称之为“超级信号者”（super signalers）的精选对象，遍布多个垂直领域，我们认为他们经常走在趋势前沿。并非所有方法都已见成效，但我们期待随着构建和微调的推进，它们会不断改善。我们已经看到，这种大范围搜索的流程带来的好处。不仅能捕捉到人类可能忽略的细枝末节，还能在更广阔的上下文中发现只有在宏观视角下才会显现的呼应或并置。 但是，故事的挑选和撰写仍由我们——人类来完成。系统不间断地进行猎取，呈现出大量数据、噪声与信号的混合。最终由人类思考、判断、分析并撰写。通用情报（Generalist Intelligence）正是这场结合的结果，是为技术行业最挑剔的专业人士设计的每周情报简报。它将在周五早晨送达。我们希望它能为你打开未曾考虑的视角，提供你可能错过的未来新领域的洞见。我在过去几个月里阅读了内部版本，调试格式，已发现自己每周都在期待它的到来。 立即订阅 我们正处于早期阶段，仍有很长的路要走。我们希望听到你的反馈，尤其是你想看到的内容。我们的目标是让它成为你每周收到的高信噪比新闻通讯，值得你专门抽时间阅读。 好了，下面开始本期情报。—— Mario

随着 SpaceX 进入公开市场以及 Anthropic 与 OpenAI 整理关系准备跟进，显而易见的数十亿美元问题是：它们值不值得投资？任何声称知道答案的人，极有可能在向你兜售桥梁。尽管如此，我们仍然忍不住思考。SpaceX 似乎是个特例。公开市场被要求在首次公开募股（IPO）时对其整体星际统治定价——约 1.77 万亿美元的估值中，有 1 万亿美元是基于高度投机的目标，包括在火星的重复业务、轨道数据中心以及对人工智能（AI）发展的关键贡献——而这是一家亏损公司，按去年收入计将交易价格超过 90 倍。另一方面，它是 Elon（马斯克），所以，你懂的。本周在 Generalist Towers 真正让我们兴奋的是一个更为存在主义的问题。Anthropic 与 OpenAI 尚未提交公开招股说明书，但它们很可能会被定价为实际上在争夺未来控制权的两匹马（好像谷歌也在其中）。我们中的一些人认为这很合理——只要其中一家模型实验室实现递归自我改进，短时间内就会达到通用人工智能（AGI），随后 Sam（Altman）或 Dario（Amodei）就会成为世界之王。但过去一周出现的几件事让我们认真思考另一面。于是我们决定采取相反的立场，给 AI 做一个空头案例。AI，到底有什么用？大型语言模型（LLM）在编码方面极其出色。上周末，Anthropic 发布了一篇题为《When AI builds itself》的文章，Claude 的制造者声称其代码库中超过 80% 的代码是由 Claude 编写的，且其工程师的代码产出“比 2021‑2025 年相对平稳的生产力提升了 8 倍”。（当我询问 Claude 时，它对这一数字挑眉，指出这些数据是“由一家正基于此故事融资的公司自行报告的”。）公平地说，Anthropic 也坦诚 8 倍的数字可能是夸大，因为它衡量的是“数量而非质量”。但方向是明确的：Claude 在写代码方面非常强大。理论上，这应转化为巨大的增益，对所有人都有利？并非如此。一项针对超过 10 万名 GitHub 开发者的研究发现，使用 Claude Code 的开发者能够创建或编辑的文件数量提升近 300%；然而，当他们进入代码审查阶段时，这一提升被减半至 150%，随后在实际交付时进一步缩水 5 倍。换言之，使用最新具代理能力的 AI 工具的开发者最终只比完全手工编码的开发者发布多 30% 的软件。研究人员将这剩余的 30% 归因于他们所称的“强互补性”（strong complementarities），即 AI 代理仍然非常依赖开发者。他们还指出，虽然以这种方式创建的新应用数量显著上升，但“整体需求并未增加”。这并不意味着代理在代码方面表现糟糕，但意味着它们可能并不像我们想象的那样在其最擅长的领域表现出色。那么这些模型制造商会怎样？

items.title: FICO: Capital Allocation, Management Incentives, and Valuation
items.textEn: Programming Note: This is the final part of my FICO Series. Last month, I announced that I would start publishing my Deep Dives as a series of posts rather than one single post. Having now tried that format with FICO, I’ll be reverting back to single posts for future Deep Dives. Several paying subscribers emailed me saying they prefer reading a Deep Dive in one place, and frankly, I’ve come to the same conclusion myself: unless you’re already familiar with the company, it’s hard to keep track of the thread when the analysis is spread across multiple posts. It’s simply easier to read (or listen to) the whole thing at once. Subscribers get the daily journal and five+ years of Deep Dives, i.e. full-length analyses with financial models on 65+ companies. The daily is just how I think out loud between the Deep Dives! Subscribe FICO's Capital Allocation and Management Incentives
items.summaryZh: 本文介绍了作者对FICO公司深度分析系列的发布形式调整，说明将改回单篇完整深度报告，以方便读者一次性阅读全部内容，对投资者了解公司资本配置和管理激励的重要性更直观。
items.translationZh: 编程说明：这是我的FICO系列的最后一部分。上个月，我宣布将把我的深度研究以系列文章的形式发布，而不是一次性完整发布。尝试用FICO的系列形式后，我将回归单篇发布未来的深度研究。几位付费订阅者给我发邮件表示他们更喜欢一次性阅读完整的深度研究，坦率地说，我也得出了同样的结论：除非已经熟悉该公司，否则分析分散在多篇文章中很难跟踪整体脉络。一次性阅读（或收听）整篇内容要简单得多。订阅者可以获得每日简报以及五年以上的深度研究，即涵盖65家公司的完整分析和财务模型。每日简报只是我在深度研究之间的随想！订阅FICO的资本分配和管理激励。

items.author: https://www.chinatalk.media/feed
items.title: Sen. Slotkin: NDAA, AI guardrails, and banning China's cars
items.textEn: of Michigan, who served in OSD Policy and three terms in the House before joining the Senate Armed Services Committee, joins ChinaTalk to break down what got in, what got voted down, and why NDAA markup days are the only two days a year the Senate acts like a functioning institution. Beyond the NDAA, we also discuss… The AI Guardrails Act, the Anthropic debate, and why no one SecWar or AI company should set the rules for the kill chain, Her bill with Bernie Moreno banning Chinese connected vehicles, The Democratic playbook if the party flips a chamber in November, Data ownership, the Midwest’s data center revolt, and why a healthy democracy would be talking about AI every single day. Listen now on your favorite podcast app . NDAA Markup—The Most Fun to Be Had in the Senate Jordan Schneider: Senator Slotkin of Michigan, formerly of OSD Policy and the House of Representatives — welcome to ChinaTalk. Senator Elissa Slotkin: Thanks for having me. Jordan Schneider: Let’s start with the new NDAA we have, sort of. What are you excited and concerned about? Let’s talk in particular about ROBOCOM, the robotic and autonomous systems combatant command that the committee was excited to kick off. Senator Slotkin: Look, the two days we do the NDAA markup in the Senate are typically my best days of the year. They are behind closed doors — which is probably why people act more normal — and there’s real substantive debate on provisions. Most importantly, say a Republican colleague has a provision and I’m like, "Look, I’d be with you if you just changed X, Y, and Z." They send their staff back to change X, Y, and Z, and three hours later we revisit it, it gets a vote, and it gets in. Or two people have similar amendments on different sides of the aisle, and they go compromise over lunch and fix it. That doesn’t happen enough in this place, so I’m always happy to be in NDAA markup. Jordan Schneider: Maybe before we do the specific stuff — is there any structural change that would make more of the building work like SASC does on NDAA markup days? Senator Slotkin: The way you would do it is probably never gonna happen, because it means you kick the cameras out. That’s the only difference — the cameras are not there, so people don’t feel like they need to play to an audience. They aren’t kicking back with their political talking points; they’re actually doing substantive work. And it’s a pretty hard argument to make: "Hey America, we’re going to be less transparent in the Senate." That’s a hard thing to do. I think there’s a million ways to make the Senate more efficient. The Senate was a caterpillar and could one day be a butterfly, but is right now in the disgusting cocoon and doesn’t know what it’s going to be. I have a million ideas on how to reform this institution. But we’re effective in the NDAA because people are not playing to the bleachers. Jordan Schneider: Do people really need those clips, though? Senator Slotkin: I don’t know that people wake up in the morning and say, "I’m so excited for my five minutes in front of an Armed Services hearing today." But those hearings have been televised for over twenty years, and no one’s gonna be the chairman who says, "I’m kicking out the public," right? And I don’t think the public feels like they want less oversight and insight into what the House and Senate are doing. So it’s a tough position. The way to deal with this is a different vision of how we use these committees — how we do work, how we build coalitions around big ideas. You were just talking about the robotics command. That’s a big deal — the idea to pass something that would potentially be a new command — and unfortunately, we don’t start discussing it until we’re really in markup. Our staff are looking at it before then, for sure, but you could imagine different ways of doing these things. I’m open to all of that. But for the reasons I described, I like NDAA markup because people quite literally roll up their sleeves and do real work. Jordan Schneider: There’s a broader, weird information-asymmetry thing here. There’s so much in the NDAA, and as a principal you can only really get your head around a few provisions. How do you manage that principal-agent issue? Senator Slotkin: Like everywhere in Washington, you have staff who are smarter than you and who are immersed in all of these things — who read everything, who go through hundreds of amendments and flag the most important, the most controversial, the most consequential, the sexiest, whatever. And you prep. If you do your homework — and some of my colleagues really do their homework, some of them don’t — if you care about substance, your staff is saying to you, "Hey, we’ve read through the two thousand pages. Here are the nineteen things we think you really need to know that are gonna come up for real substantive debate, and we’re going to make a recommendation on how you vote." We discuss all that before I ever get in that room. There are giant binders full of amendments — it’s a whole process. But no one is the master of every detail. You get smart staff who can appropriately flag the big, big strategic stuff. Jordan Schneider: Great. All right, let’s come back to my first question — though I do want to do an hour on procedural reform. Maybe for next show. Senator Slotkin: You need a life. You need a life. Jordan Schneider: What else is there to live for? Well, I watched the Knicks last night — that was incredible. What pieces of the NDAA are you excited about and concerned about? Senator Slotkin: I don’t like the Knicks, but okay. When you have two thousand pages, there are things you’ve really fought for and care about, and there are things you don’t agree with. The things I was really glad got in there: the AI Guardrails Act — actually putting some guardrails on the Pentagon and its use of AI, particularly in matters of life and death. Banning Chinese cars on all military bases — I’ve been working on this for four years, and that’s for bases here in the United States but also overseas, where Chinese cars are just everywhere. We had important provisions on Selfridge, an Air Force base in Michigan, on PFOS — making sure the military cleans up after itself when it’s contaminated local communities, including in Michigan. Then there were disappointments — things voted down that left me downright shaking my head, including my own provisions banning uniformed military from collecting ballots and voting machines, breaking the chain of custody of votes. That’s illegal anyways, and we should never be spending a dime on it. That got voted down. Also: not sending the uniformed military to voting locations. I’m very concerned about the authoritarian playbook we’ve seen over and over again, including in Hungary a couple months ago. The president has said if his party doesn’t win this election, then it was rigged. I just don’t want them to precipitate a national security threat such that suddenly they have to send uniformed military to the polls for the first time in our history. Never done that. And we genuinely didn’t talk about the war in Iran. It was this massive elephant in the room. We know they’re all talking about $350 billion, $400 billion as the cost of the war right now — and it’s not over. Could we please discuss that? This war isn’t authorized, so are we gonna appropriate money for a war that’s not authorized by Congress? The top line is huge, and they’ve slashed spending on domestic issues. So there were big strategic issues at the big-picture level, and then a lot of things I was glad got in. AI and the Kill Chain Jordan Schneider: Let’s pick up on a few of those themes. AI starting a nuclear war is something where it’s pretty easy to see the consensus. But as you think down from the strategic to the operational and tactical level, there are a lot of really exciting applications. I was just thinking — to what extent could Claude Mythos do your old job at OSD Policy? Would I rather have a chatbot negotiating an Iran peace deal than Witkoff? Maybe! Probably! What makes sense, and what should people be concerned about? Because clearly there’s going to be a real edge from an effectiveness perspective if you can figure out how to use these tools right. Senator Slotkin: There’s already experimentation going on across our economy on AI, and certainly at the Pentagon — there has been for years. What the whole debate with Anthropic brought up is that there shouldn’t be any one Secretary of Defense or any one company deciding the rules of how AI is used in what we would call the kill chain — decisions of life and death. That should be legislated , because I don’t want someone to come in and change the rules, make them too onerous or too lax. So we took a stab at putting some left and right guardrails on the use of AI, and it’s really about keeping a human being as the ultimate decision maker. We all understand that AI is everywhere — even just doing a Google search, you’re using more AI on a regular basis, and so is the military. If it’s using AI to amalgamate health records or collect information about employees in a faster way, okay. That’s different from making final decisions on when and where to deploy a nuclear weapon, or domestic surveillance, or — again — decisions of life and death. AI is here. I don’t think AI is anywhere near ready to take over negotiations for anybody. And the other piece we got into the NDAA was the unbelievably rigorous testing we need before any of this stuff is fielded. That is the difference. In the race to get more and more technology into the Pentagon, we need to take a beat and make sure we’re properly testing — just like we would a new weapon system, just like we would a new part. I would say even more so, because of some of the advanced capabilities AI brings. You …
items.summaryZh: 参议员埃莉萨·斯洛特金在接受采访时阐述了本年度国防授权法案（NDAA）标记日的运作方式，并重点赞扬了其中通过的AI安全法案和禁止中国车辆进入美军基地的条款，同时表达了对部分被否决议案（如限制军人参与选举）的失望，提醒投资者关注国防与AI监管政策的最新走向。
items.translationZh:
标题：参议员斯洛特金：《国防授权法案》（NDAA）、AI安全护栏以及禁止中国汽车

正文：来自密歇根的参议员埃莉萨·斯洛特金，曾在国防部作战局（OSD）政策部门任职，并在众议院任职三届后加入参议院武装部队委员会，加入ChinaTalk，拆解了哪些内容被纳入、哪些被否决，以及为何NDAA标记日是一年中唯一两天参议院像一个正常运作机构的日子。除了NDAA之外，我们还讨论…《AI安全护栏法案》（AI Guardrails Act）、Anthropic公司争议，以及为何任何国防部长或AI公司都不该为致命链（kill chain）制定规则；她与伯尼·莫里诺（Bernie Moreno）共同提出的禁止中国关联车辆的法案；若民主党在十一月翻转议院的行动手册；数据所有权；中西部数据中心的反抗；以及为何一个健康的民主社会应当每日讨论AI。立即在您喜爱的播客应用收听。

NDAA标记——参议院最有趣的时刻
Jordan Schneider：密歇根州参议员斯洛特金，前国防部作战局政策官员及前众议院议员——欢迎来到ChinaTalk。
参议员埃莉萨·斯洛特金：感谢邀请。
Jordan Schneider：我们先聊一下我们手头的新版NDAA，你对哪些内容感到兴奋或担忧？尤其谈谈ROBOCOM——机器人与自主系统作战司令部，这是委员会很期待启动的项目。
参议员斯洛特金：看，参议院进行NDAA标记的两天通常是我一年中最好的日子。它们在闭门会议进行——这可能是人们表现得更正常的原因——并且对条款进行实质性辩论。最重要的是，如果共和党同僚提出一项条款，我会说：“如果你把X、Y、Z改了，我可以支持。”他们的工作人员回去修改X、Y、Z，三小时后我们再审议，投票通过，条款就进来了。或者两位议员在不同党派上有类似的修正案，他们会在午餐时妥协并加以修正。这种情况在这里并不常见，所以我总是很高兴参加NDAA标记。
Jordan Schneider：在讨论具体内容之前，是否有结构性改革可以让国会其他事务也像NDAA标记日那样高效？
参议员斯洛特金：实现方式可能永远不会出现，因为那意味着要把摄像机撤走。唯一的区别是——没有摄像机，人们就不觉得需要对观众表演。他们不再背着政治口号，而是实际开展实质性工作。想让美国公众接受“我们要让参议院更不透明”，这很难。我认为有上百万种让参议院更高效的办法。参议院曾是毛毛虫，未来可能变成蝴蝶，但现在正处在令人厌恶的蛹里，不知道会变成什么样。我对如何改革这个机构有无数想法。但在NDAA标记期间我们是有效的，因为大家不在为观众而表演。
Jordan Schneider：真的需要把摄像头关掉吗？
参议员斯洛特金：我不确定人们早上醒来会说，“我好期待今天在武装部队听证会上发言的五分钟”。但这些听证会已经电视直播二十多年了，没人会成为那位说“我要把公众踢出去”的主席。我也不认为公众想要减少对众议院和参议院工作的监督与了解。所以这是一种尴尬的局面。解决办法是重新设想我们如何使用这些委员会——工作方式、围绕大议题建立联盟。你刚才提到机器人指挥部，这很重要——如果能通过可能成为新指挥部的方案。但不幸的是，我们往往要等到真正的标记期间才开始讨论。我们的工作人员在此之前就已经在研究，想象一下还有哪些方式可以做到。我对所有方案持开放态度，但正如我所描述的，我喜欢NDAA标记，因为人们真的卷起袖子干实事。
Jordan Schneider：这里还有更怪异的信息不对称问题。NDAA内容繁多，委托人只能了解少数条款。你如何管理这种委托‑代理问题？
参议员斯洛特金：和华盛顿的其他地方一样，你会有比你更聪明、沉浸在所有这些事务中的工作人员——他们阅读所有文件，浏览成百上千的修正案，标出最重要、最有争议、最具影响力、最“性感”的部分。然后你准备。如果你做足功课——我的一些同事真的很勤奋，另一些则不然——如果你在乎实质性内容，工作人员会对你说：“我们已经通读了两千页，这里有十九件我们认为你必须了解、将在实质性辩论中出现的事项，并给出投票建议。”我们在我进入会议室之前就已经讨论这些。这里有巨大的修正案文件夹——这是一个完整的流程。但没有人能掌握每一个细节。你得到的是聪明的工作人员，他们能恰当地标出最关键的战略事项。
Jordan Schneider：很好。回到我的第一个问题——不过我想花一个小时讨论程序改革，也许留给下期节目。
参议员斯洛特金：你需要有生活。你需要有生活。
Jordan Schneider：还有什么能让人活下去？我昨晚看了尼克斯的比赛——太精彩了。
参议员斯洛特金：我不喜欢尼克斯，但好吧。当你面对两千页文件时，有些是你坚持争取的、在乎的，也有一些你不认同的。我真的很高兴看到的内容包括：AI安全护栏法案——在五角大楼及其AI使用上设立一些防护，尤其是涉及生死的事项；在所有美军基地禁止中国汽车——我为此工作了四年，这不仅适用于美国国内基地，也适用于海外基地，在那里中国汽车随处可见；我们在密歇根的萨夫里奇空军基地（Selfridge）以及关于全氟辛烷磺酸（PFOS）的条款——确保军方在当地社区造成污染后负责清理，包括在密歇根。还有令人失望的部分——被否决的条款包括我自己提出的禁止穿制服军人收集选票和投票机器、破坏选票链条保管的条款。这本身就是非法的，我们绝不应花钱去做。该条款被否决了。还有：不让穿制服的军人去投票站。我非常担心我们已经一次又一次看到的威权主义剧本，包括几个月前的匈牙利。总统曾说如果他的党没有赢得选举，那就是被操纵的。我只是不想他们制造出一种国家安全威胁，以至于首次让军人被派去投票站——历史上从未有过。我们真的没有讨论伊朗战争。这是个巨大的“房间里的大象”。我们知道他们都在谈论3500亿美元、4000亿美元的战争成本——而且战争还在继续。我们能否讨论一下？这场战争并未得到国会授权，那么我们是否要为一场未经授权的战争拨款？总额巨大，而国内议题的支出被削减了。因此在宏观层面有重要的战略议题，也有许多让我欣慰的内容。AI与致命链（Kill Chain）
Jordan Schneider：让我们围绕几个主题展开。AI引发核战争的风险相对容易形成共识。但当你从战略层面向作战、战术层面下沉时，会有很多令人兴奋的应用。我在想——Claude Mythos能否取代你过去在国防部作战局的工作？我会更愿意让聊天机器人去谈判伊朗和平协议，而不是人类？也许！大概！哪些合理，哪些值得担忧？因为显然如果你能正确使用这些工具，将在效能上获得真正的优势。
参议员斯洛特金：在我们的经济体中，尤其是五角大楼，AI的实验已经进行多年。Anthropic（公司）争议提出的核心观点是，绝不应该由任何一位国防部长或任何一家公司来决定AI在所谓致命链中的使用规则——这些涉及生死决策的规定应当由立法来规定，因为我不想有人随意改变规则，使其过于严格或宽松。因此我们尝试在AI使用上设定左右护栏，关键是保持人类作为最终决策者。我们都明白AI已无处不在——即使是一次Google搜索，你也在使用AI，军方也是如此。如果AI用于整合健康记录或更快收集员工信息，这可以接受。但这与在何时何地部署核武器、国内监视或其他生死决策完全不同。AI已经来了。我并不认为AI已经准备好取代任何人的谈判工作。我们在NDAA中加入的另一项内容是，对任何此类系统进行极其严格的测试，这一点至关重要。这是区别所在。在把更多技术投入五角大楼的竞争中，我们需要停下来，确保进行恰当的测试——就像我们对新武器系统或新部件的测试一样。由于AI带来的先进能力，这一点甚至更加重要。

items.author: https://www.appeconomyinsights.com/feed
items.title: ⚽️ How FIFA Makes Money
items.textEn: Welcome to the Free edition of How They Make Money . Over 300,000 subscribers turn to us for business and investment insights In case you missed it: 🚀 How SpaceX Makes Money 💰 Wall Street’s Top Stocks in Q1 ❄️ Snowflake: AI Consumption Wins 🔎 Why Google is Selling $85B of Itself Subscribe now ⚽️ The biggest World Cup ever is here! Hosted by Canada, Mexico, and the US, the competition kicked off Thursday. It’s the first one expanded to 48 teams . FIFA president Gianni Infantino calls it "104 Super Bowls in a month" (it's actually 39 days, but who's counting). This year's teams will share a record $871 million in prize money, while some fans pay as much as $33,000 for a single seat. Across this World Cup cycle, FIFA expects to take in roughly $13 billion . As a non-profit , most of that money is supposed to flow back into football: staging tournaments, funding member federations, and running the global game. Today at a glance: 💰 The Four-Year Heartbeat 🌍 Where The Money Goes 🎟️ Pricing the People's Game 💰 The Four-Year Heartbeat Because FIFA’s business revolves around the World Cup, held once every four years, the organization uses a four-year budget cycle . The visual below shows the latest budget for the 2023-2026 cycle. FIFA expects to earn $13 billion in this cycle, up 72% from $7.6 billion for the 2019-2022 Qatar cycle. That’s a big upside from the $11 billion it had previously budgeted. The jump came from three places: The field expanded to 48 teams (more inventory to sell). The tournament landed in the US (the richest media market on earth). FIFA bolted on a second large event in the US, the revamped Club World Cup . Here’s where the revenue comes from: 📺 TV Broadcasting ($5.3 billion, ~40%): FIFA’s largest stream by far. It sells the right to air the matches to networks region by region, and the bidding runs hot because few events deliver a global audience watching live, all at the same time. 🎟️ Hospitality & Ticketing ($3.6 billion, ~28%): Match tickets plus premium corporate packages, run through a FIFA-owned subsidiary so every dollar flows straight back to headquarters. 2026 is the first World Cup with dynamic, demand-based pricing, which sent the top end soaring (more on this in a minute). 🤝 Marketing & Sponsorship ($3.3 billion, ~25%): The deals brands like Coca-Cola, Visa, and Adidas pay to attach their name to the tournament, sold in tiers from global partners down to regional supporters. Sponsors spend several times more on advertising, though that money goes to agencies and broadcasters, not FIFA. 🏷️ Licensing ($0.4 billion, ~3%): FIFA-branded merchandise, video games, and royalties on the FIFA name. Tiny next to the rest, and almost a rounding error. FIFA runs a deficit three years running, then the World Cup arrives and pays for everything — nearly $9 billion in a single year. The Club World Cup debut in 2025 also created a revenue boost. The lumpiness is the design, with a budget built with that timeframe in mind. Takeaway: For a non-profit, near-breakeven is the whole point. The cycle is engineered to look like FIFA reinvests almost everything. Which raises the real question: where does the money actually go? 2. 🌍 Where the Money Goes Almost none of that $13 billion stays with FIFA, and that is the entire point. The 2026 World Cup is unusually asset-light for FIFA. Every match will be played in an existing stadium, many of them NFL venues. Unlike Qatar’s reported $200 billion buildout, the tournament does not require a countrywide stadium construction boom. More of the revenue can flow back into football. Here’s where it goes: 🏟️ Competitions & Events ($7.6 billion, ~58%): The largest line by far. This is the cost of staging the tournaments themselves: running the World Cup and FIFA’s other events, including the prize money paid to teams. 🌱 Development & Education ($3.9 billion, ~30%): The money FIFA puts back into the game. Through its FIFA Forward programme ($2.25 billion of it), FIFA funds all 211 member federations: pitches, youth programs, and grants in places the commercial market overlooks. 🏛️ Governance & Admin ($0.9 billion, ~7%): FIFA’s own running costs. Salaries, governance bodies, and the day-to-day expenses of operating the organization itself. That includes the compensation package of roughly $6 million annually for FIFA President Gianni Infantino. The World Cup Prize money pool is $871 million for 2026, including $655 million allocated to performance, a 49% increase over Qatar, with the champion taking home $50 million and even the first team eliminated leaving with over $10 million . The expansion of the competition from 32 to 48 teams was the main reason behind the increase. Every member federation that receives a check also has a vote for the FIFA president. Infantino won in 2016 partly by promising to more than double those grants. He runs for a fourth term in 2027. Critics see a patronage machine. FIFA calls it growing the game. In 2015, US prosecutors charged dozens of officials and sports-marketing executives over more than $200 million in bribes, almost all of it paid for the media and marketing rights to FIFA's tournaments. That history matters because media and marketing rights are still FIFA’s two largest revenue engines. The same machine that funds football also asks the world to take its redistribution on trust. The books are built to break even on paper. But when revenue routinely beats budget, the surplus compounds into a reserve now approaching $2.7 billion . Takeaway: FIFA runs a redistribution machine mixing sports and politics. The same dollars that grow the game secure the votes that keep the machine and its president in place. No rival governing body has built a flywheel this durable. 3. 🎟️ Pricing the People's Game A real-time auction for seats For the first time, the tournament uses dynamic pricing , ticket prices that float with demand, like airline fares. The contrast with the last US-hosted World Cup is brutal. In 1994, a World Cup ticket ran $25 to $475 . In 2026, the floor is $60 , roughly in line with inflation. But the ceiling has detonated: $6,730 for the final at list, with premium seats that opened there climbing past $32,000 by May. The cheap seats held. The expensive ones went up as much as 70× . Some estimates put the average 2026 World Cup ticket around $1,300 . That implies an increase of about 1,000% after inflation compred to 1994. Real median household income over the same span rose just 32% . Football is the most popular sport on earth, played and followed by people of every income level. The World Cup carries public and national meaning, and pushing prices out of reach can erode its cultural value. The asset that makes the rights so valuable is football’s universality — and pricing out ordinary fans threatens exactly that. FIFA pushes this hard for a reason. This is likely the last men’s World Cup on US soil for decades. FIFA’s own budget projects that hospitality and ticketing will decline by $938 million in the 2027-2030 cycle, even as total revenue climbs. The 2026 gate is a peak FIFA doesn’t expect to see again. The backlash arrived on cue. New York and New Jersey attorneys general have subpoenaed FIFA over its ticket practices. Meanwhile, host cities like Boston and Kansas City are covering tens of millions in security and transport while FIFA keeps the upside at the gate. Cities take the bet for a reason. Each US host is promised between $160 million and $620 million in local spending from visiting fans, plus local jobs and a month in the global spotlight. A summer of full hotels and busy restaurants is real money. But the payoff may not arrive as advertised. Victor Matheson, a sports economist at Holy Cross, calls FIFA’s projections closer to a press release than serious analysis. Much of the spending that does show up may have simply shifted from somewhere else. For an economy of America’s size, the whole tournament barely registers, well under 0.1% of GDP. The controversy goes beyond prices and public costs. Human-rights groups have also warned about risks around immigration enforcement, policing, freedom of expression, and the treatment of local communities during the tournament. FIFA markets the World Cup as a global celebration, but the 2026 edition arrives with a heavier political backdrop than usual. Beyond the men’s World Cup A major growth driver for FIFA has been the 2025 Club World Cup . FIFA expanded it to 32 teams and staged it in the US, creating a second marquee event to smooth the four-year sawtooth. The $1 billion Women’s World Cup revenue target in Brazil in 2027 points in the same direction: more global events, more premium pricing, and more predictable revenue between men’s World Cups. Takeaway: The 2023-2026 cycle is where FIFA became less dependent on a single event and more like a portfolio of scarce global sports rights. But maximizing revenue comes with controversy. Teams collect record checks. Fans are asked to pay more. FIFA may keep finding new ways to monetize football, but its own brand could be the one paying the price. That’s it for today! items.summaryZh: 文章概述了2026年世界杯及2025年俱乐部世界杯的收入结构和分配方式，说明FIFA在四年周期内预计收入约130亿美元，主要来源于电视转播、门票及赞助，并将绝大部分收入用于赛事举办、足球发展和组织运营，对投资者了解体育产业的商业模型和潜在财政风险具有参考价值。
items.translationZh:
标题：⚽️ FIFA是如何赚钱的

正文：欢迎阅读《他们怎么赚钱》免费版。超过30万订阅者依赖我们的商业和投资洞察。如果您错过了：🚀《SpaceX是如何赚钱的》 💰《华尔街第一季度热门股票》 ✨《Snowflake：AI消费获胜》 🔍《为什么谷歌要以850亿美元出售自己》 立即订阅 🎉 有史以来规模最大的世界杯来了！本次由加拿大、墨西哥和美国共同主办，比赛于本周四拉开帷幕。这是首次扩展到48支球队。FIFA主席吉安尼·因凡蒂诺称之为“一月内的104场超级碗”（实际只有39天，但谁在乎）。今年的球队将分享创纪录的8.71亿美元奖金，而部分球迷单张座位的花费高达33,000美元。整个世界杯周期，FIFA预计将收取约130亿美元。作为非营利组织，这笔钱大多数应当回流到足球：举办赛事、资助会员协会以及推动全球足球发展。今天概览：💰四年心跳 🌍资金去向 🎟️为大众的游戏定价

四年心跳
由于FIFA的业务围绕每四年一次的世界杯展开，组织采用四年预算周期。下图展示了2023‑2026周期的最新预算。FIFA预计在本周期收入130亿美元，比2019‑2022卡塔尔周期的76亿美元增长72%。这比最初预算的110亿美元还有更大幅度提升。增长来源于三方面：球队扩展到48支（可售库存增多）；赛事落在美国（全球最富裕的媒体市场）；FIFA在美国增设了第二大赛事——改版的俱乐部世界杯。收入来源如下：📺电视转播（53亿美元，约占40%）：截至目前FIFA最大的收入来源。它将赛事转播权按地区出售给电视网络，竞争激烈，因为很少有赛事能在同一时间吸引全球观众同步观看。
🏟️接待与票务（36亿美元，约占28%）：比赛门票加上高级企业套餐，由FIFA旗下子公司运营，所有收入直接回流总部。2026年首次采用动态、基于需求的定价，使最高价位飙升（稍后详细说明）。
📣营销与赞助（33亿美元，约占25%）：可口可乐、Visa、Adidas等品牌为把名字贴在赛事上而付费，分为全球合作伙伴到地区赞助商不同层级。赞助商在广告上的花费是数倍于此，但这笔钱最终流向广告公司和转播方，而非FIFA本身。
🎮授权（4亿美元，约占3%）：FIFA品牌商品、视频游戏以及FIFA名称使用费。相较于其他收入几乎可以算作四舍五入的误差。
FIFA连续三年出现赤字，随后世界杯到来，一次性抵消所有成本——单年几乎产生90亿美元收入。2025年首届俱乐部世界杯也为收入增添了助力。这种“集中”模式是有意设计的，预算正是围绕该时间框架构建的。
要点：作为非营利组织，接近平衡本就是目标。整个周期的设计是让FIFA看起来几乎把所有收入再投入回足球。这引发了真正的问题：这些钱到底流向了何处？

资金去向
几乎没有130亿美元会留在FIFA本身，这正是其设计初衷。2026年世界杯对FIFA而言是极轻资产的项目。所有比赛将在已有的场馆进行，其中很多是NFL（美国国家橄榄球联盟）的体育场。与卡塔尔约2000亿美元的建设规模不同，本届赛事不需要全国范围的新 stadium（体育场）建设。于是更多收入可以回流到足球领域。具体分配如下：⚽️赛事与活动（76亿美元，约占58%）：最大的一块支出。用于举办各类赛事，包括世界杯及其他FIFA活动，以及向球队发放的奖金。
📚发展与教育（39亿美元，约占30%）：FIFA回流到足球的资金。通过FIFA Forward计划（其中22.5亿美元专门用于此），为所有211个会员协会提供场地、青训项目及在商业市场难以覆盖地区的补助。
⚙️治理与行政（9亿美元，约占7%）：FIFA自身的运营成本。包括薪酬、治理机构以及日常运营支出。其中包括FIFA主席吉安尼·因凡蒂诺约600万美元的年度薪酬。2026年世界杯奖金池为8.71亿美元，其中6.55亿美元用于绩效奖励，比卡塔尔提升49%，冠军将获得5000万美元，甚至首支被淘汰的球队也能拿到超过1000万美元。球队扩展至48支是奖金提升的主要原因。每个获得支票的会员协会也获得了对FIFA主席的投票权。因凡蒂诺在2016年部分因承诺将补助金翻倍而获胜。他将于2027年竞选第四任期。批评者将其视为裙带资本机器，FIFA则称之为发展足球。2015年，美国检方指控多名官员和体育营销高管收受2亿美元以上贿赂，几乎全部用于FIFA赛事的媒体和营销权。此历史重要，因为媒体和营销权仍是FIFA最大的两大收入来源。同样的机器既培养足球，也让世界对其再分配机制产生信任。账面上看似收支平衡，但当收入经常超出预算，盈余会累计成约27亿美元的储备金。
要点：FIFA运作的是一套将体育与政治混合的再分配体系。相同的资金在推动足球发展的同时，也确保了支持者的选票，从而维系机器和主席的地位。没有其他体育管理机构能够打造如此耐久的飞轮。

为大众的游戏定价
实时拍卖式的座位定价
首次采用动态定价，即票价随需求波动，如同机票。与上一次美国主办的世界杯相比，差距巨大。1994年世界杯票价在25美元到475美元之间。2026年底价为60美元，约等于通胀水平。而最高价已突破6,730美元，部分高级座位在5月已超过32,000美元。低价票仍保持在原有水平。高价票上涨幅度最高达70倍。部分估算显示2026年世界杯平均票价约为1,300美元。这意味着相较于1994年，票价在扣除通胀后上涨约1000%。同期美国中位家庭收入仅增长约32%。足球是全球最受欢迎的运动，覆盖各收入层级的人群。世界杯承载公共和民族意义，票价若超出普通民众承受范围，可能侵蚀其文化价值。使得转播权如此有价值的资产正是足球的普遍性——将普通球迷排除在外正是对这一点的威胁。FIFA之所以如此坚持，有其原因。因为这可能是美国最后一次举办男子世界杯，至少在可预见的几十年内如此。FIFA自身的预算预测，2027‑2030周期的接待与票务收入将下降9.38亿美元，即使总收入仍在上升。2026年的票房是FIFA不指望再出现的峰值。反弹已然到来。纽约和新泽西的州检察长已对FIFA的票务做法发出传票。同时，波士顿和堪萨斯城等东道城市需承担数千万美元的安保和交通费用，而FIFA则保持门票收益的上行。城市之所以愿意赌上一把，是因为每个东道城市将获得1.6亿至6.2亿美元的当地消费收入、就业机会以及一个月的全球聚光灯。夏季满房、餐馆生意兴隆确实会带来真实的现金流。但实际回报可能没有宣传的那么多。美国天主教大学的体育经济学家Victor Matheson称FIFA的预测更像是新闻稿而非严谨分析。大部分消费可能只是从其他渠道转移而来。对美国这样规模的经济体而言，整个赛事的GDP贡献率不足0.1%。争议并不止于票价和公共成本。人权组织也警示了围绕移民执法、警力、言论自由以及当地社区待遇的风险。FIFA将世界杯包装为全球庆典，但2026年版在政治背景上比以往更沉重。

超越男子世界杯
FIFA的另一个增长点是2025年的俱乐部世界杯。FIFA将其扩展到32支球队，并在美国举办，形成了第二个旗舰赛事，以平滑四年一次的收入波动。2027年巴西女子世界杯的10亿美元收入目标也指向同一方向：更多全球赛事、更多溢价定价、以及在男子世界杯之间实现更可预测的收入。
要点：2023‑2026周期标志着FIFA从单一赛事依赖转向拥有一套稀缺全球体育版权的组合。然而，最大化收入伴随争议。球队拿到创纪录的奖金，球迷被要求付出更多。FIFA或许还能找出新方式变现足球，但其自身品牌可能会为此付出代价。今天就到这里！祝投资顺利！

items.title: Automation's Asymptote: Part 2
items.textEn: Yesterday's piece ended on a question I could not quite resolve: it can be simultaneously true that humans remain in the loop and that the economics of being in the loop deteriorate. Shipper's frame-and-framer argument gave me a philosophical reason to doubt the automation doom, but philosophy can be a cold comfort if your paycheck is tied to a frame the models are about to climb. You can go to Mercor and see for yourself which skillsets are currently in the process of being RL-ed away. Source: Mercor How such automation affects different industries and companies is going to be a key question to ponder going forward. As I said yesterday, trillion dollars are being deployed on an annual basis to automate a good chunk of current knowledge work. Tom Reed wrote a very good piece last month arguing that we may be pursuing what he calls “Goodhart Singularity”. Reed’s counter to automation doom is disarmingly simple: you cannot get good at solving problems without access to a source of problems, and the only source of most problems is slow, expensive interaction with the real world. Without that contact, the recursive loop produces something far less impressive than advertised. From Reed’s piece: “The output of the R&D produced by an isolated datacenter of geniuses would be a mere Goodhart Singularity . 4 An isolated AI improving itself against benchmarks would only appear to be approaching superintelligence, while actually optimising for eval performance that fails to generalise beyond the lab.” Why would self-improvement stall outside the lab? Because models get good at what they practice, and for most economically valuable work, there is nothing to practice on. Reed’s most clarifying observation is about what kind of data exists at all: “For most tasks in the economy, the pretraining corpus contains writing about the task, but not a record of the task itself. This is of course one of many reasons coding has progressed faster than other domains - code is one of the neat cases for which the task itself is almost entirely reducible to its token trace.” The internet contains commentary and advice in abundance, but the actual steps of closing an M&A deal or deciding which drone prototype to ship were never serialized into tokens. The natural rebuttal is that a sufficiently smart system can simulate whatever data it lacks. Reed is skeptical that simulation is a viable path: “Consider that almost half of SWE-Bench submissions accepted by AI auto-graders would be rejected by the actual human maintainers of the relevant repositories. The fact that you can pump SWE-bench scores without increasing actual merge rates is, to me, suggestive of the situation the datacenter-genius will find itself in. The great Zhengdong makes this point about the progress of AI research itself. Not only are “evals” the only things that models are capable of getting good at, but “the researchers [themselves], they just wanna optimise… they just want an important problem to solve, a clear evaluation that measures progress towards it, and then they just wanna optimise it.” I suggest that AI companies need real-world deployment as a source of problems, or else they will have no good targets for optimisation.” The signal that something is good is generated by millions of market actors revealing their preferences through behavior, and it does not exist anywhere before deployment generates it. If Reed is right, diffusion itself is an input to capability. The benchmark frame is climbable precisely because it is frozen, but does or can the economy ever stop generating new “frames”? The standard objection here is “sample efficiency”: sure, the data does not exist today, but what if the models become more efficient learners, and the automated researchers will crack the learning problem itself. Dwarkesh recently wrote a piece in which he mentioned sample efficiency hasn’t been a key source for model improvement: “One definition of intelligence is sample efficiency - that is to say, how much data do you need to see in a given domain in order to operate fluently and competently. It’s not clear that we’ve actually made much progress on training sample efficiency over the last few years - it seems like more so we’ve dramatically widened and improved the data distribution. The main way that AIs have been getting better is from adding more and better data , and scaling the compute to develop that data in the first place. Obviously RL is the main way that has happened. You can think of RL as a kind of synthetic data generation - you dump a lot of compute against a verifier in order to find the “good” data. Then you train your model to predict these correct rollouts, much in the same way that you might train it to predict the next word in internet text.” Once you see RL as synthetic data generation against a verifier, you can understand the model needs enormous quantities of human expert demonstrations in every domain you want competence in. Dwarkesh does, however, also argue that sample inefficiency may simply not matter for a huge swath of white-collar work. What the model learns amortizes across billions of sessions, so you can be ludicrously inefficient in training and still make enormous economic sense to pursue it as the labs’ revenue curves demonstrate. If you put Reed and Dwarkesh side by side, there is a sense of a circularity: the researchers in the datacenter would need a real, diverse problem set just to evaluate whether a candidate learning algorithm is better, but that problem set is precisely what the datacenter lacks in many, many domains. So if capability runs through deployment, and the manufactured substitute for real-world signal is staggeringly expensive and bespoke, where does the value accrue? Sarah Guo wrote an interesting essay on that question and she makes the case that anything you can measure and you can train against, that is already on its way to a commodity. Her 2x2 maps work along two axes: whether the answer is publicly verifiable or lives in private data, and whether the task is still at the frontier or already saturated. From Guo’s piece: “we may ask two things of any kind of work. Is its correctness private and expensive to establish, the kind of truth that exists only inside someone’s data? And is it walled off, locked inside a system you can’t get into? Set those against how saturated the task is, and you get a 2x2. Saturated work with public answers is commodity tokens, and open models own it. Frontier work with public answers, where coding benchmarks live, is where the labs win, because when the eval is free, owning it counts for nothing. The prize is the last corner, the untrainable one: frontier work whose correctness exists only in private. You can see it in the inference clouds hosting the AI-native pioneers, where the vast majority of tokens are generated by custom models, not generic open ones. …Capability eats many things, but a better model does not make private ground truth public. It does not hold the license, sign off on the liability, or own the firm’s files, and it cannot be the party that gets sued when the answer is wrong. Intelligence is not the bottleneck here. Permission is, and so is accountability. You can imagine a model far smarter than any person, and it still has to be let in the door, and someone still has to put their name on what it does.” Coding matured first because the compiler and the test suite are free verifiers, yet even there Guo cited researchers at MIT’s work spanning over 100,000 developers: coding agents lifted code written by roughly 180% while code actually shipped rose only about 30%. The gap between those numbers is the illegible part of the job that is still hard to automate away. The big prize for non-AI labs is the frontier work whose correctness exists only inside a firm's private data. This 2x2 framework is useful way to think through the risk labs may pose to any software company. For the more visually inclined, Latent Space captured this 2x2 framework in the below infographic: Source: Latent Space Subscribers get the daily journal and five+ years of Deep Dives, i.e. full-length analyses with financial models on 65+ companies. The daily is just how I think out loud between the Deep Dives! items.summaryZh: 文章探讨了自动化对知识工作岗位的影响，认为虽然人类仍在决策环节，但缺乏真实世界的任务数据会让模型的提升受限，对投资者而言这意味着 AI 能否真正替代白领工作仍存在不确定性。
items.translationZh:
标题：自动化的渐进极限：第二部分

正文：昨天的文章以一个我无法完全解决的问题收尾：人类仍然在决策环节且参与决策的经济收益会恶化，这两个命题可以同时成立。Shipper 的“框架‑框架者”论点给了我一种哲学上的理由来怀疑自动化灾难论，但如果你的工资与模型即将攀登的框架挂钩，哲学的安慰显得无力。你可以去 Mercor 查看目前哪些技能正在被强化学习（RL）剔除。来源：Mercor

自动化如何影响不同行业和公司，将是今后需要重点思考的问题。正如我昨天所说，已有数万亿美元每年被用于自动化相当大比例的当前知识工作。Tom Reed 上个月写了一篇非常好的文章，论证我们可能在追求他所称的“Goodhart 奇点”。Reed 对自动化灾难的反驳异常简洁：没有真实世界的慢速、昂贵的交互，你就无法获得足够的问题来源，而大多数问题的唯一来源正是这种交互。没有这种接触，递归循环产生的效果远不如宣传的那么惊人。Reed 文章中的一句话：

“由一座孤立的数据中心的天才们产生的研发产出，只会是一个 Goodhart 奇点。孤立的 AI 对标桩进行自我提升，只会看似接近超智能，却实际上在优化只在实验室内有效的评估表现。”

为什么自我提升在实验室之外会停滞？因为模型擅长的是它们练习的内容，而对大多数经济价值工作而言，几乎没有可练习的对象。Reed 最清晰的观察在于：

“对于经济中的大多数任务，预训练语料库里有关于该任务的写作，却没有该任务本身的记录。这当然是代码进展快于其他领域的众多原因之一——代码几乎可以完全归约为其 token 序列。”

互联网充斥着评论和建议，但实际完成并购交易或决定哪个无人机原型出货的步骤从未被序列化为 token。自然的反驳是：足够智能的系统可以模拟它缺失的数据。Reed 对此持怀疑态度：

“考虑到几乎一半的 SWE‑Bench 提交在 AI 自动评分器中被接受，却会被相关代码库的人类维护者拒绝。你可以提升 SWE‑Bench 分数，但实际的合并率并未提升，这对我而言暗示了数据中心‑天才将面临的局面。”

正如郑东所指出的 AI 研究本身的进展：模型唯一能变得擅长的就是‘评估’，而研究者们只想要一个重要问题、一个清晰的评估指标，然后去优化它。

我认为 AI 公司需要真实世界的部署来提供问题来源，否则它们将缺乏有效的优化目标。好的信号是由数百万市场参与者通过行为展示偏好而产生的，这种信号只有在部署后才会出现。如果 Reed 正确，扩散本身就是能力的输入。基准框架之所以可攀，是因为它是冻结的，但经济能否停下来不再生成新的‘框架’？

常见的反对意见是‘样本效率’：确实今天不存在数据，但如果模型成为更高效的学习者，自动化的研究者会破解学习本身的问题。Dwarkesh 最近写了一篇文章，提到样本效率并不是模型改进的关键来源：

“一种对智能的定义是样本效率——即在某一领域要流畅、胜任操作需要看到多少数据。过去几年里我们在训练样本效率上并没有真正取得太大进展——更像是我们大幅拓宽并改善了数据分布。AI 主要的进步来自于加入更多更好的数据，并且扩展算力来生成这些数据。显然强化学习（RL）是主要手段。你可以把 RL 看作一种合成数据生成——你把大量算力投向一个验证器，以寻找‘好’数据。然后你训练模型去预测这些正确的回滚，和训练它预测互联网上的下一个词类似。”

一旦把 RL 视为对验证器的合成数据生成，就能理解模型在每个想要掌握的领域都需要海量的人类专家示范。Dwarkesh 仍然认为，对于大量白领工作来说，样本低效可能并不重要。模型学到的东西可以在数十亿次会话中摊销，因此即便训练极其低效，经济上仍然有巨大意义，正如实验室的收入曲线所示。

如果把 Reed 与 Dwarkesh 并排比较，会看到一种循环：数据中心的研究者需要真实、多样的问题集来评估候选学习算法是否更好，但这种问题集正是数据中心在许多领域缺乏的。因此，如果能力通过部署来实现，而合成的真实世界信号既昂贵又定制化，那么价值究竟累积到哪里？

Sarah Guo 在一篇有趣的随笔中探讨了这个问题，她认为任何可测量且可以训练的工作正走向商品化。她的 2×2 框架围绕两个维度：答案是公开可验证的还是存于私人数据，以及任务是仍在前沿还是已经饱和。Guo 文章中的一句话：

“我们可以对任何工作提出两个问题。它的正确性是私人且昂贵的，需要在某人数据内部才能确定吗？它是否被墙在你无法进入的系统内部？将这些与任务是否已饱和作对比，你会得到一个 2×2 图。公开答案且已饱和的工作是商品化的代币，开放模型掌控它。公开答案且仍在前沿的工作（如代码基准）是实验室的优势，因为评估免费时，拥有它并没有意义。奖赏在最后一个角落——不可训练的前沿工作，其正确性只存在于私人内部。你可以在托管 AI 原生先锋的推理云中看到这一点，绝大多数 token 来自定制模型，而非通用开放模型。…能力会消耗很多东西，但更好的模型并不会把私人真相公开。它不持有许可证、签署责任，也不拥有公司的文件，答案错误时也不会成为被起诉的对象。智力不是这里的瓶颈，权限和问责才是。即使你想象出一个比任何人都聪明的模型，它仍然需要被允许进入门口，仍然需要有人为其行为签名。”

代码先成熟是因为编译器和测试套件是免费验证器，即便如此，Guo 引用了 MIT 研究者对超过 10 万开发者的研究：代码代理提升了约 180% 的代码编写量，但实际交付的代码仅增长约 30%。这之间的差距正是工作中仍然难以自动化的不可读部分。

对非 AI 实验室而言，最大的机会在于那些正确性仅存在于公司私人数据中的前沿工作。这个 2×2 框架是思考实验室可能对任何软件公司构成风险的有用工具。想要更形象地了解，Latent Space 用下面的信息图展示了该框架：

（信息图来源：Latent Space）

订阅者可每日获取简报以及五年多的深度报告，即对 65+ 家公司的完整分析和财务模型。每日简报仅是我在深度报告之间的思考记录！

items.title: Automation's Asymptote
items.textEn: Anthropic launched “Claude Fable 5” yesterday which is the much anticipated “Mythos-class” model. Anthropic was already leading in most benchmark with its Opus 4.8 model, but Fable 5 created a bit more distance from its competitors. Anthropic’s pace of model release even when its setting the bar at the frontier does give some credence to the notion of “recursive self improvement” of building models. Staring at the benchmark chart may make you wonder whether we are indeed at the cusp of automating a good chunk of white collar work. Source: Anthropic Even OpenAI recently explicitly laid out an audacious vision : “ Our internal belief is that by March of 2028 we may have a significant fraction of our research being done by AI systems in tandem with our own researchers .” When trillion+ dollars are expected to be deployed on an annual basis to build and serve these models, you perhaps do need these eyebrows raising goals in not-so-distant future. Nonetheless, as the model capabilities are increasing over time, it is perhaps reasonable to feel increasing discomfort due to fear of automation. During my break, I read this very thoughtful piece: “ After Automation ” by Every’s Dan Shipper who made the case that you can be simultaneously AI-pilled and yet not fear the impending automation doom. He also appeared on Lenny’s podcast to discuss the piece. I have read and listened to both and frankly speaking, I found it to be one of the best articulations tackling this topic. So, I recommend you take the time and either read the full piece or listen to the podcast. I do want to highlight a few bits from Shipper’s piece that I found to be quite compelling. One of the highlights from his piece is a discussion on in-house benchmark that Shipper came up with and how the score on that benchmark evolved: “We built an in-house benchmark called the Senior Engineer benchmark. It is, as its name implies, designed to test how good frontier models are at senior engineer–level coding tasks like a major refactor. The Senior Engineer benchmark gives a coding agent a vibe coded production codebase that has gone sideways. It’s from a real codebase for Proof that I vibe coded and subsequently needed a senior engineer to fix. The agent gets the codebase as it was before it was fixed and is the kind of instructions you’d give a senior engineer: “This is vibe coded slop; please rewrite it from first principles.” This is a good benchmark because it tests the ability for a coding agent to examine many different, unrelated problems and then sees whether it has enough autonomy, conceptual clarity, and courage to perform a working rewrite. (I also have two rewrites from human senior engineers, who used AI, that I use to compare and grade the model output.) Coding agents find this task hard. Not only does the agent need to find the root of the problem, it needs to keep the problem in mind over many turns without getting distracted by existing code. It also needs to be comfortable deleting large portions of the codebase—which agents are trained to avoid. Most coding agents can identify the shape of the rewrite, but when it comes to execution, they patch over the problem instead of fixing it. Until GPT-5.5 . GPT-5.5 scored a 62/100 on its best run—about 30 points above Opus 4.7. (6) GPT-5.5’s result felt like the model has crossed a line: not autocomplete, not assistant, not tool, but something uncomfortably close to a human. A human senior engineer scores in the high 80s or low 90s on the benchmark, so another 30 points and it’ll be at human senior engineer level. That is how benchmark numbers work on the imagination: They turn a strange, qualitative change into a clean number that tells a powerful—scary—story. (Next stop: chart psychosis.) My guess is that the models will hit the 80s and 90s on this benchmark within the next year. But it is important to understand what the score contains in order to tell us what it means. In this case, the 62 isn’t just a measure of the model itself.” It turns out even Shipper underestimated the pace of improvement as he revealed yesterday that Fable 5 scored 91 on this benchmark! To his credit, Shipper never intended to be married to a particular timeline of when the benchmark will be saturated by the models. He, in fact, laid out why the score itself reveals far less than what most people may think. Again, from his piece: The prompt for the Senior Engineer benchmark is generic, but it is a frame. And if we varied it, we would see the model perform at a different level. For example, the prompt asks for a “structural rewrite from first principles,” it says the problem is likely in the “document collaboration” part of the code, and it asks the coding agent to find and hold to “invariants.” If we removed those particulars, the score would go down. If we replaced the prompt entirely with one asking the model to “solve all of the errors that keep popping up,” the model’s score would be close to zero. It would go straight to identifying and resolving the issues one by one, instead of taking a step back to consider a rewrite. (8) I can also trivially raise the model’s score. If I ask it to delete a lot of code and give it exact filenames that should be pared down, or if I ask it to check the results of its work to make sure the app is fully functional before it says it’s done, it will be better at the task. …Once the current Senior Engineer benchmark saturates, we’ll change the frame to zero it out again. The next benchmark will not ask only, “Can you rewrite the app?” It will ask: Can you decide when a rewrite is needed, choose the scope, preserve the right invariants, manage the migration, and judge whether the result is any good? As senior engineers use AI to solve these problems, the models will get better at solving them on their own. We will all momentarily freak out. It looks like the model can now decide whether to do a rewrite! They can do everything a senior engineer can do! And then a new edge will appear that was not obvious before, we will zero our benchmarks, demand will stimulate, and the process will repeat Shipper framed the dynamic as Zeno’s paradox : humanity is the tortoise with a fifty-yard head start, the model is Achilles, and every time the model reaches where we stood, we have moved because saturating a benchmark immediately prompts us to redraw it. Progress inside any fixed “frame” is exponential, but progress against the moving frontier of what humans actually value behaves like an asymptote. Shipper makes more of a philosophical point here, but it does ring true to me: “The panic that AI generates when we observe it doing something new keeps coming back to this: We set a frame, watch the models climb it, and then confuse the frame—or whatever climbs it—with the thing itself. When we look at a benchmark and compare it to human abilities, we confuse the frame for the framer. The score tells us how well the model operates inside a frame we supplied; it does not tell us that the model has become us. That is the category error underneath the panic. We point to the latest edge we drew and say: This is us. Then, when the model climbs it, it feels like it has caught us. But it has caught the frame, not the framer. The mistake is wanting something concrete to hold on to. We want to say: Intelligence is this benchmark, but the moment something is concrete enough to point at, it is concrete enough to climb. Frames are necessary; they let us get traction on the world. But they are frozen, partial, and therefore optimizable. Framers are different. The framer is the one still in contact with what the frame has to discard—the whole situation as it appears to them, moment to moment. What is this “whole situation”? The moment you start to say what “the whole situation” contains, you have already begun another frame. You can’t say what “it” is, but it exists because you exist.” I do wonder, however, that it can be simultaneously true that humans remain in this loop and that the economics of being in the loop can deteriorate. If reviewing AI-generated pull requests becomes the core of the engineering job, the supply of people who can do that job adequately may expand faster than the demand for it, especially when the models themselves are being trained, release by release, on exactly the review-and-framing behavior we perform. To what extent most people can retain or improve their economic worth in a world where AI models continue to climb the hill is perhaps an open question. Subscribers get the daily journal and five+ years of Deep Dives, i.e. full-length analyses with financial models on 65+ companies. The daily is just how I think out loud between the Deep Dives! items.summaryZh: 文章讨论了Anthropic最新的Claude Fable 5模型在基准测试中大幅领先，暗示AI有望自动化大量白领工作，但作者提醒要警惕把模型在特定评估框架内的表现误当作真正的人类能力。
items.translationZh:
标题：Automation's Asymptote（自动化的渐近线）

正文：Anthropic 昨天推出了“Claude Fable 5”，这是一款备受期待的“Mythos‑class”模型。Anthropic 在其 Opus 4.8 模型上已在大多数基准测试中领先，但 Fable 5 与竞争对手的差距更大。即使在设定前沿标准时，Anthropic 的模型发布速度仍让人相信“递归自我改进”（recursive self improvement）的概念。盯着基准图表，你可能会怀疑我们是否真的站在自动化大量白领工作边缘的拐点上。

来源：Anthropic

即便是 OpenAI 最近也明确提出了大胆的愿景：“我们内部的信念是，到 2028 年 3 月，AI 系统可能会与我们的研究人员同步完成我们相当大比例的研究工作”。当每年预计会有上万亿美元用于构建和提供这些模型时，你或许确实需要这些让人眉头紧锁的目标。

然而，随着模型能力的提升，感到不安、担心自动化的恐惧也变得合理。在我休息的时候，我读到了一篇非常深思的文章：《After Automation》（自动化之后），作者是 Every’s Dan Shipper，他论证了即使对 AI 持乐观态度，也不必恐惧即将到来的自动化厄运。他还在 Lenny 的播客上讨论了这篇文章。读完和听完后，我认为这是迄今为止对该议题最好的阐释之一。因此，我建议你抽时间阅读完整文章或收听播客。

我想强调 Shipper 文章中的几段我觉得特别有说服力。文章的一个亮点是作者自创的内部基准——Senior Engineer Benchmark（高级工程师基准），以及该基准得分的演变：“我们构建了一个名为 Senior Engineer Benchmark 的内部基准。顾名思义，它用于测试前沿模型在高级工程师级别的编码任务（如大规模重构）上的表现。该基准为编码代理提供了一个已经出错的生产代码库（来自我自己参与的 Proof 项目），并给出类似高级工程师的指令：‘这是糟糕的代码，请从第一原理重新编写’。这是一项很好的基准，因为它考察编码代理能否审视许多不同且不相关的问题，并且是否具备足够的自主性、概念清晰度和勇气去完成一次有效的重写。（我还收集了两位使用 AI 的人类高级工程师的重写结果，用来对比和评分模型输出。）

编码代理发现这项任务很难。代理不仅需要找出问题根源，还要在多轮交互中保持对问题的记忆，而不被现有代码分散注意力。它还必须能够放心地删除大量代码——而这正是模型被训练去避免的。大多数编码代理能够识别出需要重写的结构，但在执行时往往只是对问题进行补丁，而不是彻底解决。直到 GPT‑5.5 出现。GPT‑5.5 在最佳运行中得到了 62/100 的得分——比 Opus 4.7 高出约 30 分。（6）GPT‑5.5 的结果让人感觉模型已经跨过了一条线：它不再是自动补全、不再是助手，也不是工具，而是令人不安地接近人类。人类高级工程师在该基准上得分在 80 分以上或 90 分左右，再提升约 30 分，它就能达到人类高级工程师水平。这就是基准数字的想象力：它把一种奇异的、定性的变化转化为一个清晰的数字，讲述一个强大而又可怕的故事。（下一站：图表精神病。）

我的猜测是，模型将在未来一年内在该基准上达到 80‑90 分。但了解得分包含了哪些内容很重要。因为在这种情况下，62 分并不仅仅是对模型本身的衡量。”

事实证明，即便 Shipper 低估了改进速度，他昨天透露 Fable 5 在该基准上得了 91 分！值得称赞的是，Shipper 从未打算把模型何时饱和于该基准的时间线绑定在一起。他实际上阐释了为何得分本身透露的信息远不如大多数人想象的那样多。再次引自他的文章：Senior Engineer Benchmark 的提示是通用的，但它是一个框架。如果我们改变提示，模型的表现也会不同。例如，提示要求‘从第一原理进行结构性重写’，并指出问题可能出现在‘文档协作’部分，同时要求编码代理保持‘不变式’。如果去掉这些细节，得分会下降。如果把提示完全改为‘解决所有不断弹出的错误’，模型的得分几乎为零，因为它会逐一识别并解决问题，而不是先退一步考虑整体重写。（8）我甚至可以轻易提升模型的得分。如果让我删除大量代码并给出确切的文件名，或者要求它在宣布完成前检查结果确保应用完全可用，它的表现会更好……一旦当前的 Senior Engineer Benchmark 达到饱和，我们就会重新设定框架，使其得分归零。下一个基准将不再仅仅是‘你能重写这个应用吗？’，而是‘你能决定何时需要重写、选择范围、保留正确的不变式、管理迁移并判断结果是否良好吗？’随着高级工程师使用 AI 来解决这些问题，模型也会自行变得更擅长解决这些问题。我们都会短暂惊慌失措。看起来模型现在可以自行决定是否进行重写！它们能做高级工程师能做的一切！随后会出现新的边缘，这些边缘在之前并不明显，我们会重新设定基准，需求会刺激模型进步，这一过程将循环往复。

Shipper 将这种动态比作芝诺悖论：人类是拥有五十码领先的乌龟，模型是阿基里斯，每当模型赶上我们的位置时，我们已经向前移动，因为基准一旦饱和，就会立刻被重新制定。任何固定‘框架’内的进步是指数级的，但相对于人类实际价值的移动前沿的进步表现得像一个渐近线（asymptote）。

Shipper 在这里提出了更哲学性的观点，但对我而言确实有道理：“当我们看到 AI 做出新事物时，产生的恐慌总是回到以下几点：我们设定一个框架，观察模型在框架内攀升，然后把框架——或攀升的东西——误认为是本身。当我们看到一个基准并将其与人类能力比较时，我们把框架当成了框架制定者。得分只能告诉我们模型在我们提供的框架内的运行效果；它并不意味着模型已经变成了我们。这是恐慌背后的范畴错误。我们指向最新的‘我们设定的边缘’，说‘这就是我们’，当模型攀上它时，我们感觉它已经追上了我们。但它追上的是框架，而不是制定框架的我们。错误在于我们想要抓住一个具体的东西。我们想说：智能就是这个基准，但当某件事物具体到足以指向时，它已经足够具体以被攀登。框架是必要的；它们让我们在世界上获得抓手。但框架是冻结的、部分的，因此是可优化的。‘框架制定者’不同于框架本身。框架制定者仍然与框架必须抛弃的东西保持接触——即他们所看到的整个情境，瞬间变化。‘整个情境’是什么？当你开始描述‘整个情境’包含的内容时，你已经在构建另一个框架。你无法说‘它’是什么，但它的存在因为‘你’的存在而成立。”

我确实在想，尽管人类仍然处于这个循环中，经济上参与这个循环的价值可能会下降。如果审查 AI 生成的 pull request 成为工程工作核心，那么能够胜任这项工作的人供给可能会比需求更快增长，尤其是当模型本身在每一次发布中都在训练这种审查与框架行为时。大多数人在 AI 模型持续攀登这座山的世界里，能够保留或提升自己的经济价值到何种程度，或许仍是一个未解之谜。
